extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
**Thermal Incubator System (Cyber-Physical System - CPS)**
  - **Type**: Controlled thermal environment system for maintaining precise temperature regulation.
  - **Main Components**:
    - **Physical Twin (PT)**:
      - Insulated styrofoam box (thermal insulation).
      - Heatbed (active heating element).
      - Fan (air circulation for even heat distribution).
      - **Temperature Sensors**:
        - 2 internal sensors (monitoring temperature inside the styrofoam box).
        - 1 external sensor (monitoring ambient temperature outside the box).
    - **Cyber Components**:
      - Raspberry Pi (embedded controller for real-time temperature regulation).
      - Low-level driver (abstracts sensor/actuator communication).
      - Communication server (RabbitMQ for message synchronization).
      - Controller (bang-bang control logic with configurable parameters).
  - **Operational Domain**:
    - Temperature regulation range: **Lower Limit (LL) and Upper Limit (UL)** (exact values not specified but critical for control).
    - Dynamic thermal inertia modeling (focus on heatbed behavior).
    - Real-time feedback loop for adaptive control.
  - **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
    - **Thermal inertia modeling**: Two simulation models (Model A: 2 free parameters; Model B: 4 free parameters) for predicting heatbed dynamics.
    - **Environmental adaptability**: Robustness to changes in internal content (e.g., added cold water).
    - **Safety-critical**: Temperature thresholds enforced via controller limits (LL, UL) and safe protocols in the low-level driver.
  - **Domain Context**: Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) integration with emphasis on thermal management and real-time control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
**Digital Twin Services for the Incubator**:
  - **Monitoring**:
    - **Real-time visualization**: Dashboard with live temperature data (via time-series database and RabbitMQ messages).
    - **Data collection**: Sensor data (internal/external temperatures) streamed via low-level driver to RabbitMQ.
  - **Optimization**:
    - **On-demand calibration**: Automated calibration of simulation models (Model B) using historical/real-time data (Kalman filter integration).
    - **Controller parameter tuning**: What-if simulations to optimize controller parameters (e.g., heating duration `H`, waiting duration `C`) using co-simulation with historical data.
  - **Prediction**:
    - **Anomaly detection**: Kalman filter estimates heatbed temperature (non-measured) from sensor data, detecting deviations from expected behavior.
    - **Thermal inertia prediction**: Model B (4-parameter) outperforms Model A (2-parameter) in capturing heatbed dynamics.
  - **Control**:
    - **Self-adaptation loop**: Dynamic reconfiguration of the controller when system behavior changes (e.g., added cold water):
      1. Anomaly detection via Kalman filter.
      2. Data collection experiment (cooling/heating phase).
      3. Model calibration (Model B) with new parameters.
      4. Kalman filter reconfiguration.
      5. What-if simulations for optimized controller behavior.
  - **Target Users**:
    - Engineers, researchers, and operators requiring real-time thermal management insights.
    - Applications in medical devices, food preservation, or experimental setups.
  - **Capabilities**:
    - **Robustness**: Reconfigurability via calibration and self-adaptation.
    - **Predictability**: Simulation-based forecasting of temperature trends.
    - **Safety**: Enforced temperature limits (LL, UL) and safe protocols in the low-level driver.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
**Technologies and Frameworks**:
  - **Communication Infrastructure**:
    - **RabbitMQ**: Open-source message broker for real-time synchronization between:
      - Low-level driver (sensor/actuator communication).
      - Controller (temperature regulation logic).
      - Digital Twin components (data visualization, calibration, anomaly detection).
    - **Protocol**: Safe communication protocols in the low-level driver to prevent unsafe states.
  - **Simulation and Modeling**:
    - **Simulation Engines**:
      - Two thermal models for the heatbed:
        - **Model A**: 2 free parameters (simpler, less accurate).
        - **Model B**: 4 free parameters (better inertia prediction, used for Kalman filter).
    - **Calibration Method**: Automated process to update model parameters using real-time sensor data.
  - **Control Systems**:
    - **Controller**: Bang-bang control strategy with configurable parameters:
      - **LL**: Lower temperature threshold.
      - **UL**: Upper temperature threshold.
      - **H**: Heating duration.
      - **C**: Waiting duration (delay to prevent overshoot).
    - **Statechart**: Visual representation of controller states (Fig. 8).
  - **Data Processing**:
    - **Time-series Database**: Stores real-time sensor data for visualization and analysis.
    - **Kalman Filter**: Estimates heatbed temperature from sensor data (Model B integration).
  - **Development Tools**:
    - Open-source hardware/software repository: [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator).
    - **CPS Framework**: Integration of cyber (computation) and physical (thermal) components.
  - **Enablers for Digital Twinning**:
    - **Real-time Data Streaming**: RabbitMQ enables low-latency communication between PT and DT.
    - **Modular Architecture**: Components (low-level driver, controller, communication server) decoupled for DT extension.
    - **Self-adaptation Capabilities**: Closed-loop design for dynamic reconfiguration.
    - **Visualization**: Dashboard for interactive monitoring (time-series data plotting).

extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
**Development and Modeling Phase:**
    - **Initial Model Creation**: Abstract representations of the incubator system are developed to capture static properties (e.g., physical dimensions, sensor placements) using CAD-like models (e.g., styrofoam box, heatbed, fan). These models are **low-fidelity** and focus on structural aspects (e.g., shapes, insulation properties) but lack dynamic behavior (e.g., thermal dynamics).
    - **Dynamic Model Development**: Two simulation models (Model A: 2 free parameters; Model B: 4 free parameters) are created to predict temperature dynamics inside the incubator. Model B is refined to better capture the **thermal inertia of the heatbed**, enabling higher fidelity simulations.
    - **Calibration Phase**: Models undergo **on-demand calibration** using real-time data from the Physical Twin (PT) to adjust parameters dynamically (e.g., via a tracking simulator referenced in [15]). This phase bridges the gap between static and dynamic representations.

    **Operational Phase (Digital Twin Deployment):**
    - **Real-Time Monitoring**: The Digital Twin (DT) continuously collects data from sensors (e.g., 3 temperature sensors: 2 inside the box, 1 outside) via a **low-level driver** and communicates via RabbitMQ. Data is stored in a **time-series database** for visualization (e.g., dashboards).
    - **Anomaly Detection & State Estimation**: A **Kalman filter** (Model B) correlates sensor data with the heatbed’s temperature (non-measured) to detect anomalies and estimate states in real-time.
    - **Self-Adaptation Loop**: The DT implements a **closed-loop adaptation** for dynamic adjustments:
      1. Detects changes in system behavior (e.g., new objects like cold water buckets).
      2. Runs experiments (e.g., controlled cooling/heating cycles) to gather data.
      3. Recalibrates Model B and updates the Kalman filter with new parameters.
      4. Executes **what-if simulations** to optimize controller parameters (e.g., via co-simulation with historical/real-time data).
      5. Reconfigures the controller (e.g., adjusting LL, UL, H, C parameters in the statechart).

    **Lifecycle Governance:**
    - **Version Control**: Open-source hardware/software specifications are hosted on GitHub ([2]), enabling collaborative development and auditing.
    - **Transition Management**: The DT evolves incrementally by integrating new models (e.g., Model B), algorithms (e.g., Kalman filter), and calibration methods without discarding prior models. The **RabbitMQ communication server** facilitates seamless integration of DT components with the PT.
    - **Quality Assurance**: Experiments validate model accuracy (e.g., Model B’s superior prediction of heatbed inertia) and DT functionality (e.g., anomaly detection, controller optimization).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
**Engineering Methodologies:**
    - **Hybrid Modeling Approach**: Combines **physical models** (e.g., thermal dynamics) with **cyber-physical integration** (e.g., control logic, sensor data). Models are developed at varying abstraction levels (e.g., Model A: simplified; Model B: detailed).
    - **Calibration-Driven Evolution**: DTs use **automated calibration** (e.g., tracking simulator) to update models in real-time. This is exemplified by the **on-demand calibration** process in the incubator case study.
    - **Co-Simulation Framework**: DTs leverage **co-simulation** (e.g., real-time/historical data) to validate controller parameters and optimize performance (e.g., via what-if simulations).

    **Technologies and Protocols:**
    - **Communication**: RabbitMQ (message broker) enables real-time data exchange between the PT (Raspberry Pi controller) and DT components (e.g., low-level driver, monitors).
    - **Control Logic**: A **bang-bang controller** with parameters (LL, UL, H, C) regulates temperature. The DT refines this logic via adaptive algorithms (e.g., Kalman filter).
    - **Simulation Tools**: Two simulation models (Model A/B) are implemented to predict temperature dynamics. Model B’s parameters are recalibrated dynamically during the self-adaptation loop.
    - **Testing/Validation**:
      - **Model Validation**: Experiments compare Model A/B’s accuracy (Model B outperforms Model A in capturing heatbed inertia).
      - **DT Validation**: Anomaly detection (Kalman filter) and controller optimization (what-if simulations) are validated via real-world experiments.

    **Version Control and Evolution**:
    - **Open-Source Platform**: Hardware/software specifications are available on GitHub ([2]), enabling reproducibility and community contributions.
    - **Incremental Evolution**: DTs evolve by adding layers (e.g., calibration, anomaly detection) without replacing core models. The **RabbitMQ server** ensures interoperability between PT and DT components.
    - **Quality Assurance**: Rigorous testing (e.g., calibration experiments, state estimation) ensures DT reliability. The **self-adaptation loop** validates dynamic adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
**Decision Support Capabilities:**
    - **Real-Time Visualization**: DT provides **live dashboards** (via time-series databases) to monitor incubator status (e.g., temperature profiles, sensor data).
    - **Anomaly Detection**: The Kalman filter identifies deviations from expected behavior (e.g., temperature spikes due to new objects), triggering alerts or adaptive actions.
    - **State Estimation**: Estimates unmeasured variables (e.g., heatbed temperature) using Model B, improving control precision.
    - **What-If Simulations**: DT simulates controller configurations (e.g., adjusted LL, UL, H, C) to predict outcomes before deployment, optimizing performance (e.g., energy efficiency).
    - **Self-Adaptation Loop**: Automates decision-making for dynamic scenarios:
      1. Detects anomalies (e.g., cold water bucket).
      2. Runs experiments to gather data.
      3. Recalibrates models (e.g., Model B) and updates the Kalman filter.
      4. Optimizes controller parameters via simulations.
      5. Configures the controller for the new scenario.

    **Analytics and Processing:**
    - **Data Processing**: Sensor data (3 temperature sensors) is processed via low-level drivers and RabbitMQ for real-time transmission.
    - **Model-Based Analytics**: Models (Model B) and algorithms (Kalman filter) generate insights (e.g., thermal inertia, state estimates).
    - **Confidence Measures**: Calibration accuracy (e.g., Model B’s superior fit) and anomaly detection thresholds (e.g., Kalman filter thresholds) provide confidence in recommendations.
    - **User Interaction**: Dashboards and RabbitMQ-based messaging enable human operators to intervene (e.g., adjust controller parameters) or automate actions (e.g., trigger experiments).

    **Decision-Making Workflow**:
      1. **Observation**: DT monitors PT (e.g., temperature sensors).
      2. **Analysis**: Anomalies detected via Kalman filter; thermal inertia estimated via Model B.
      3. **Action**: DT schedules experiments, recalibrates models, and optimizes controllers.
      4. **Feedback**: Controller parameters are reconfigured based on simulation results.

directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

specifications of the case study.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
